prompt: The passage is organized mainly as

choices.text: a side-by-side comparison of two competing rival inventors
choices.rationale: No two inventors are compared.

choices.text: a set of numbered instructions for building a bicycle at home
choices.rationale: No build instructions are given.

choices.text: a chronological account of how one invention developed over time
choices.rationale: Correct. It proceeds from 1817 through later improvements to the 1890s.

choices.text: an argument warning readers against modern forms of transportation
choices.rationale: It does not argue against transportation.

explanation: The structure is a chronological development story.

stimulus.title: The Machine That Walked
stimulus.text: The earliest ancestor of the bicycle had no pedals at all. Built in 1817, it was a wooden frame on two wheels that a rider straddled and pushed along with the feet, like walking while seated. People mocked it, and rutted roads made it impractical, so it soon faded. Decades passed before inventors added pedals, then a chain, then air-filled tires that smoothed the ride. Each change drew new riders, and by the 1890s the bicycle had become a craze. It offered something rare for the time: cheap, independent travel that needed no horse and no rails. For many people, especially those long denied easy movement, the machine quietly widened the boundaries of daily life.

prompt: The phrase 'the bargain is uneasy' marks a shift from

choices.text: telling a personal story to listing a set of instructions
choices.rationale: No instructions follow.

choices.text: describing a clear benefit to acknowledging a real danger
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage pivots from fertile-soil benefits to the danger overhead.

choices.text: praising the region's farmers to sharply criticizing them
choices.rationale: The passage does not criticize farmers.

choices.text: discussing volcanic ash to discussing the distant oceans
choices.rationale: Oceans are never discussed.

explanation: The phrase signals the turn from benefit to risk.

stimulus.title: Gifts of the Volcano
stimulus.text: It seems strange that people would farm in the shadow of a volcano, but many do, and for good reason. When a volcano erupts, it scatters ash rich in minerals such as potassium and phosphorus. Over years, this ash breaks down into some of the most fertile soil on Earth. Crops planted in it can grow unusually well, which is why slopes near certain volcanoes are crowded with farms and villages. The bargain is uneasy: the same mountain that feeds the fields can also threaten them. Farmers weigh the steady gift of rich soil against the rare but real danger overhead, and generation after generation, many choose to stay.

prompt: The author includes the word-list experiment mainly to

choices.text: provide concrete evidence that sleep helps the brain store memories
choices.rationale: Correct. The experiment supports the memory-consolidation claim.

choices.text: describe a reliable method for falling asleep more quickly
choices.rationale: No sleep-onset advice is given.

choices.text: argue that studying before a test is, on the whole, pointless
choices.rationale: The passage does not dismiss studying.

choices.text: compare two unrelated regions of the human brain in detail
choices.rationale: No two regions are compared.

explanation: The experiment functions as supporting evidence.

stimulus.title: The Night Shift of the Brain
stimulus.text: Sleep can look like idleness, but the brain is busy. During deep sleep, waves of electrical activity sweep across the cortex, and researchers think these waves help move the day's memories into longer-term storage. In one line of experiments, students who slept after studying a list of words remembered more of them than students who stayed awake the same number of hours. Sleep may also clear away molecular waste that builds up while we are awake. None of this means more sleep is always better; the relationship appears to follow a curve, with both too little and too much linked to poorer performance. What the brain seems to need is enough, at the right times.

prompt: The comparison to human accents primarily serves to

choices.text: help readers understand how song dialects can drift over time
choices.rationale: Correct. The analogy clarifies generational drift in song.

choices.text: argue that making recordings of birdsong should be banned
choices.rationale: No argument about banning recordings appears.

choices.text: prove that birds and human beings are in fact closely related
choices.rationale: The passage draws an analogy, not a claim of kinship.

choices.text: list, in order, the stages of a single sparrow's life cycle
choices.rationale: It is an analogy, not a life-cycle list.

explanation: The accent comparison is an explanatory analogy for drift.

stimulus.title: Songs With Accents
stimulus.text: A white-crowned sparrow raised near San Francisco does not sing quite like one raised a hundred miles north. The basic melody is the same, but small flourishes differ from place to place, forming what biologists call song dialects. Young sparrows learn these dialects by listening to adult males during a brief window in their first months. A bird that hears no song in that window grows up to sing a thin, simplified tune. The dialects can drift over generations, much as human accents do, so a recording made decades ago may sound subtly foreign to the birds singing in the same meadow today.

choices.text: a back-and-forth debate between two rival musicians
choices.rationale: There is no debate between characters.

choices.text: a numbered list of tips for choosing a good violin
choices.rationale: No tips are offered.

choices.text: a moment of doubt that gradually gives way to a realization
choices.rationale: Correct. It moves from Nadia's hesitation to her concluding insight.

choices.text: a chronological history of one town's orchestra
choices.rationale: It is a single scene, not an orchestra's history.

explanation: The arc runs from doubt to epiphany.

stimulus.title: The Borrowed Violin
stimulus.text: Nadia had practiced on a school instrument so battered that two of its pegs slipped during every lesson. When her teacher handed her a loaner violin for the recital, she nearly refused; it felt wrong to play something so fine. But as she drew the bow across the strings, the tone opened like a window onto a bright street. Notes she had fought for all year now arrived easily, as if the instrument had been waiting for her. Afterward, returning it to its case, she understood that her old struggles had not been wasted. The fine violin had simply revealed the player she had quietly become.

prompt: The passage is organized mainly by

choices.text: comparing the tardigrade in detail with a single named rival animal
choices.rationale: No specific competitor is named.

choices.text: introducing the animal, then its survival ability, then a proposed mechanism
choices.rationale: Correct. It moves from description to ability to a tentative explanation.

choices.text: presenting and defending an argument for funding human space travel
choices.rationale: The passage does not argue for funding.

choices.text: tracing the history of tardigrade research decade by decade in order
choices.rationale: No decade-by-decade history is given.

explanation: The structure proceeds from portrait to capability to mechanism.

stimulus.title: The Survivor
stimulus.text: Smaller than a grain of salt, the tardigrade looks almost comical, plodding on eight stubby legs. Its talent, however, is anything but ordinary. When its surroundings dry out, the creature expels most of its water and curls into a husk called a tun. In this state it can endure boiling heat, the vacuum of space, and doses of radiation that would kill nearly any other animal. Years later, a single drop of water can revive it. Scientists are still untangling how the tardigrade protects its cells, but one clue is a special set of proteins that seem to harden into glass, cradling delicate structures until water returns.

prompt: The author notes that 'the cost of a book did not fall overnight' primarily to

choices.text: begin a step-by-step guide to operating an early printing press
choices.rationale: No printing guide follows.

choices.text: suggest that the scribes of the time were dishonest about their prices
choices.rationale: Scribes' honesty is never discussed.

choices.text: argue that the printing press was, on the whole, a costly failure
choices.rationale: The passage treats the press as transformative, not a failure.

choices.text: qualify the idea of instant change before describing the long-term effect
choices.rationale: Correct. The detail tempers the idea of an immediate revolution before noting the long-term effect.

explanation: The clause adds nuance, acknowledging change was real but gradual.

stimulus.title: Letters of Lead
stimulus.text: Before the 1450s, a book in Europe was copied by hand, a task that could occupy a scribe for a year. Johannes Gutenberg changed that arithmetic. By casting individual letters in metal, he could arrange them into a page, print hundreds of copies, then rearrange the same letters for the next page. The cost of a book did not fall overnight, and many scholars still prized handwritten volumes. But within fifty years, presses across the continent had produced millions of books. Ideas that once crept from city to city now traveled in bulk, and a reader in one town could hold the exact words read by a stranger far away.

prompt: Which choice best describes the overall structure of the passage?

choices.text: It lists, in order, the steps a diver should follow to observe an octopus.
choices.rationale: No how-to steps are given.

choices.text: It compares two different species of octopus and weighs their abilities.
choices.rationale: Only one animal is discussed, not two species.

choices.text: It introduces a surprising trait, explains its mechanism, then notes a deeper puzzle.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage moves from a striking fact to a mechanism to the colorblindness paradox.

choices.text: It argues that octopuses are endangered and should be protected from fishing.
choices.rationale: The passage makes no argument about protection.

explanation: The passage's purpose is to explain and then deepen a surprising fact, not to argue or instruct.

stimulus.title: The Color of Thought
stimulus.text: An octopus has no bones and, oddly, almost no color of its own. Yet in less than a second it can flush deep red with alarm or fade to match the speckled gray of a stone. Beneath its skin lie millions of tiny sacs of pigment, each ringed by muscle. When the muscles pull, the sacs widen and color blooms; when they relax, the color vanishes. Stranger still, the octopus is colorblind, so it cannot see the very hues it produces. Some researchers suspect its skin itself senses light, letting the animal paint a disguise it can never admire.
